roster.name: Wren Okafor-Silva
roster.role: Player-character protagonist of 'First Bell.' Her practiced first-day survival system collides with real choices — who to sit with, who to defend, who to become — forcing her to notice, maybe for the first time, that the performance has a cost.

roster.name: David Alvarez
roster.role: The well-meaning adult architect of the episode's central pressure points — he creates both the vulnerable spotlight moment and the forced-collaboration reckoning, not out of cluelessness but out of a genuine, slightly clumsy belief that discomfort now prevents isolation later. He's not neutral scenery; his choices actively shape the social chessboard the player has to navigate, and his own history with being the outsider quietly parallels Wren's arc without ever being stated outright.

roster.name: Alex Rivera-Chen
roster.role: Alex is the emotional stakes-bearer of 'First Bell' — the human cost that makes the player's social choices matter. Alex is not a lesson in tolerance; they're a specific, guarded kid testing whether this new place will go the way the last one did. Every branch of the episode is really a referendum on whether Wren notices Alex, and what she does with that noticing — from a passing glance in the hallway to a real, if fragile, friendship forged under pressure in the library.

roster.name: Maya Delacroix-Thompson
roster.role: Maya is the gravitational center of the popular table and the primary test of the player's spine — not a villain, but a mirror of what Wren's own survival instincts could calcify into if she never risks anything. Every scene with Maya is a referendum on whether the player mistakes magnetism for belonging.

roster.name: Jordan Oyelaran
roster.role: The warm first contact who represents 'belonging' as ease and popularity-by-proximity — a low-stakes ally whose limits (avoiding real conflict) surface right when the player needs someone to hold ground, making room for the player's own growth to matter more.
